evals.prompt: Should we go on Amazon? We're DTC-only at $8M revenue, 55% gross margin, $35 AOV.
evals.expected_output: Walks the marketplace-fit gate (Amazon passes on margin and AOV). Builds the unit economics: 15% referral + ~$4 FBA + ~25% ads on a $35 item leaves single-digit contribution. Recommends conditional yes, with hero SKUs DTC-exclusive and discovery SKUs on Amazon. Pulls margin by SKU and DTC LTV to compare against the marketplace customer-no-contact tradeoff.
assertions: Applies the marketplace-fit gate
Builds the all-in Amazon fee math at the SKU AOV
Pulls modern.sales.margin_by_product
Recommends the hero-vs-discovery SKU split
Names the customer-data ownership tradeoff
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: We're on Amazon and losing the Buy Box on our own SKU. Why?
evals.expected_output: Walks Buy Box mechanics. Five inputs: price, fulfillment method, performance metrics, inventory health, account standing. Pulls the SKU's competitor landscape and the brand's current ODR. Recommends FBA over FBM if not already, price-matching within 1-2%, fixing ODR if above 1%. Notes that brand-registered unique ASINs avoid the race-to-the-bottom dynamic.
assertions: Names the five Buy Box inputs
Asks for current ODR and inventory health
Recommends FBA if not currently used
Names the 1% ODR threshold
Notes brand registry as the structural fix
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: Since we launched on Amazon our DTC revenue is flat. Is Amazon eating us?
evals.expected_output: Walks the cannibalization measurement framework. Recommends a geo-holdout if possible, otherwise a time-series before/after with seasonality adjustment, and a customer-overlap audit. Notes 20-40% cannibalization is typical. Pulls channel revenue and cohort LTV to compare DTC customer value to marketplace customer value. Recommends accepting the cannibalization at a measured rate rather than reflexively pulling off Amazon.
assertions: Walks the four cannibalization measurement methods
Pulls modern.sales.by_channel for the time-series
Names 20-40% cannibalization as typical
Recommends overlap audit against DTC email list
Conditions the marketplace decision on net incrementality
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: Should we put all our SKUs on Amazon or just some?
evals.expected_output: Walks the SKU portfolio strategy. Hero SKUs DTC-exclusive (or limited marketplace inventory), discovery SKUs marketplace-led, bundle SKUs DTC-only. Notes that marketplace listings should subsidize DTC, not replace it. Pulls margin by SKU to identify which SKUs belong where. Warns against the everything-everywhere reflex.
assertions: Walks the SKU portfolio strategy framework
Distinguishes hero, discovery, bundle, loss-leader
Pulls modern.sales.margin_by_product to inform the split
Names the marketplace-subsidizes-DTC principle
Warns against the everything-everywhere reflex
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: Amazon is inviting us to go 1P / Vendor Central. Should we?
evals.expected_output: Distinguishes 1P from 3P. In 1P Amazon buys wholesale and controls pricing including the right to suppress your DTC floors. Right for: brands that want to offload merchandising and accept the customer-relationship hand-off. Wrong for: brands with DTC pricing discipline that cannot afford Amazon undercutting their own site. Recommends modeling the pricing impact before signing.
assertions: Defines 1P / Vendor Central vs 3P / Seller Central
Names Amazon's pricing control in 1P
Conditions the decision on DTC pricing discipline
Names the customer-relationship hand-off
Recommends pricing impact modeling pre-signature
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: We sell on Amazon but Walmart Marketplace keeps pitching us. Worth it?
evals.expected_output: Walks the Walmart fit gate. Smaller GMV than Amazon, lower competitive density in most categories. Right for: brands with strong reviews on Amazon already, broad-appeal price-conscious catalogs. Pulls the brand's existing Amazon performance as the strongest predictor. Conditions yes on existing Amazon traction; conditional no if the brand is still struggling on Amazon.
assertions: Walks the Walmart Marketplace fit gate
Conditions on existing Amazon performance
Names lower competitive density as the upside
Notes the price-conscious customer profile
Recommends evaluating Amazon traction first
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: Our category on Amazon is dominated by 30 unbranded competitors all underpricing us. What do we do?
evals.expected_output: Identifies the unbranded-category race-to-the-bottom dynamic. The fix is structural: brand registry on unique ASINs, branded packaging, differentiated SKU configuration so the Buy Box does not pit you against generic competitors. If the catalog is genuinely commodity, recommend exiting Amazon for that category and concentrating on DTC where brand-pricing discipline holds. References retail-buyer-pitch if wholesale becomes the better alternative.
assertions: Identifies the unbranded race-to-the-bottom dynamic
Recommends brand registry and unique ASIN setup
Names differentiated SKU configuration as the structural fix
Conditions on whether the catalog is genuinely commodity
References retail-buyer-pitch as an alternative channel
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move
